channel.title: XKey Updates
channel.description: XKey - Vietnamese Input Method for macOS

item.title: Version 1.2.22 (Build 20260525.1)
item.description:
What's New

fix(engine): harden undoTyping path — Quick Telex, overflow, stable entry IDs (#276)

Builds on #274 to make undoTyping() produce the user's exact original

keystrokes across every supported typing flow, and closes the invariant gaps

in TypingBuffer's keystroke tracking that previously caused undo to silently

corrupt the output.

Five concerns rolled into one change:

  1. Quick Telex undo (cc→ch, gg→gi, kk→kh, nn→ng, pp→ph,

qq→qu, tt→th): handleQuickTelex appended the replacement key

directly without recordKeystroke and stored the replacement as the

entry's primary keystroke. Typing "chó" as c-c-o-s left the sequence

with [c, o, s] and the new entry's primary as h instead of c, so

ESC restored "cos" — silently dropping the duplicated consonant for

every Vietnamese word starting with ch/gh/kh/ng/ph/qu/th. Fix appends an

entry whose primary keystroke is the user's actual key, records it in

typing-order, then overwrites processedData with the replacement for

display.

  1. Buffer overflow guard: TypingBuffer caps entries at MAX_SIZE (32)

and moves older entries to a separate overflow array, but

keystrokeSequence keeps growing — it isn't trimmed on overflow. For a

Vietnamese word longer than 32 chars, backspaceCount would only delete

32 displayed chars while undo injected 33+ keystrokes. canUndoTyping

and undoTyping now refuse undo when buffer.hasOverflow is true.

Pass-through is the safe choice; overflowed words are too rare to

justify overflow-aware restoration.

  1. Stable entry IDs for restore-then-undo: restore(from:) previously

overwrote keystrokeSequence with getAllRawKeystrokes() (per-entry

order, modifiers grouped with their owners) because removeLast popped

the sequence tail blindly. That hack discarded typing order, so after a

history restore (backspace across a word boundary) undo for a word with

non-adjacent modifiers like "thừa" (t-h-u-a-w-f) replayed "thuwfa"

instead of the user's actual "thuawf". Fix:

- Give every CharacterEntry a stable id from a lock-protected

monotonic counter (NSLock — XCTest can schedule test classes across

threads even though production typing is single-threaded; a torn

+= 1 would hand two entries the same id).

- Stamp every RawKeystroke with the owning entry's id; modifiers carry

the id of their target entry, not the entry being typed.

- addModifier(at:) / addModifierToLast return the stamped keystroke

so callers (insertStateAt) hand the same instance to

recordKeystroke.

- recordKeystroke auto-stamps unstamped keystrokes with

entries.last.id for the common case right after

append(keyCode:isCaps:).

- removeLast() / remove(at:) filter the sequence by entryId

instead of popping the tail, so they remove the entry's keystrokes

from wherever they sit in typing order.

- removeLastModifier* walk from the tail and remove the matching

keystroke by entryId + keyCode + caps, so identical modifiers on the

same entry don't confuse it.

- restore(from:) now uses snapshot.keystrokeSequence verbatim.

- id and entryId are excluded from Equatable (identity, not

value), so existing value-based tests keep working.

  1. Close remaining invariant gaps: three more places mutated the buffer

without keeping keystrokeSequence in sync with totalKeystrokeCount:

- processWordBreak spell-check restore built a temporary buffer from

keyStates and snapshotted it without ever recording keystrokes; the

resulting snapshot had non-empty entries but an empty sequence.

- restoreFromLegacy appended entries from legacy data without

populating the sequence.

- checkQuickConsonant (f→ph, j→gi, w→qu, g→ng, h→nh,

k→ch — all off by default) modified entries via buffer.clear()

+ re-append loop and via bare buffer.append(...) without recording.

All three now record each keystroke after the append so the invariant

sequence.count == totalKeystrokeCount holds.

  1. saveSpaces / saveSpecialChar: both built snapshots with an empty

sequence; they now populate the sequence from each entry's primary

keystroke so the invariant survives that restore path too.

Tests:

  • New testUndoTypingchoQuickTelexrestoresFullInput — c-c-o-s → engine

shows "chó" → undo restores "ccos".

  • New testUndoTypingbufferOverflowrefusesUndo — 33 'a' + tone mark →

canUndoTyping returns false; undoTyping returns doNothing.

  • New testUndoTypingafterHistoryRestorepreservesTypingOrder — type

"thừa", round-trip through a snapshot, ESC restores "thuawf".

  • Rewrote three RestoreEditTests cases that asserted the old per-entry

rebuild semantics; they now assert typing-order preservation.

  • Migrated testHistoryRestoreThenEditProducesCorrectRestore,

testCreateSnapshotIncludesKeystrokeSequence,

testModifierAtOldEntryMaintainsTypingOrder, and

testTypingWithLateModifierProducesCorrectRestoreOrder to the

stamped-keystroke pattern — auto-stamping inside recordKeystroke uses

entries.last.id, which would mis-label modifiers on non-last entries

unless callers pass the stamped value returned by addModifier(at:).

  • All existing undo / Quick Telex / overflow / canUndoTyping tests still

pass; full XKeyTests suite green.

Xem chi tiết trên GitHub
